title: Simple Built-In Daybed Frame
summary: A wall-mounted 2x4 slat frame with no box, drawers, or headboard — a cleat bolted to studs and three L-shaped legs carrying 2x4 slats, for a genuinely built-in daybed.
description:
This is the simplest version of a daybed frame in this catalog — no storage box, no drawers, no headboard, just a wall cleat and a handful of L-shaped legs carrying bare 2x4 slats. Build three legs for a 75"-wide daybed; if you need to span farther, add a leg roughly every 3 feet to keep the slats from sagging.

**Attach the back cleat directly to wall studs with 2-1/2" wood screws first**, then attach the legs to the cleat with pocket hole screws — this is a genuinely built-in piece, not a bed frame that happens to sit near a wall. **At least one leg, and preferably both outer legs, must land on a wall stud** — this is what keeps the whole daybed from racking side to side under someone sitting on the edge; a leg attached only to drywall anchors will not hold.

Attach the slats to the legs with 2-1/2" screws, and if you want a more finished look than bare slat ends, cover the exposed ends with plywood or wood planking — this is a cosmetic step, not a structural one.

materials.name: Wood screws, 2-1/2" or longer, for wall attachment

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut three leg back pieces at 37-3/4", three leg base pieces at 10-1/2" and seven slats at 75" from the 2x4, 8 ft or stud length. Cut three leg cap pieces at 14" from the 1x2. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to nine 8 ft 2x4, 8 ft or stud length and one 4 ft 1x2. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the three L-shaped legs
steps.body: Each leg is an L: a 37-3/4" 2x4 back piece with a 10-1/2" 2x4 base butted square to one end, joined with two 2-1/2" pocket hole screws driven from the base into the back piece. Note that 10-1/2" plus the 3-1/2" width of the back piece is exactly 14" — the length of the 1x2 leg cap — so the cap lies across the outside of the L covering both members and pinning the joint; glue it and add 1-1/4" brads. Build all three legs the same way and stack them together to check they match; three legs that differ leave the slats sitting on a twist.

steps.title: Attach the back cleat to the wall
steps.body: The back cleat is not in this plan's cut list and the nine declared 2x4 are fully consumed by the legs and slats, so buy one more 8 ft 2x4 and cut the cleat to 75", matching the slat length that sets the daybed's width. Find the studs, mark a level line at your chosen seat height, and screw the cleat flat to the wall with the 2-1/2" wood screws, at least two into every stud it crosses. Get this line level and straight before anything else attaches to it — every leg and every slat inherits it, and a cleat that sags in the middle cannot be corrected later.
materials: Wood screws, 2-1/2" or longer, for wall attachment

steps.title: Attach the legs to the cleat
steps.body: Set one leg at each end of the 75" cleat and one in the middle, roughly 37-1/2" on center, so no slat spans more than about three feet unsupported. Attach each leg's back piece to the cleat with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, keeping the top edges flush so the slats land on one plane. At least one leg and preferably both outer legs must land on a wall stud — that stud connection is what stops the daybed racking sideways when someone sits on the front edge, and drywall anchors alone will not hold it.

steps.title: Attach the slats
steps.body: Lay the seven 75" slats across the three legs, running the full width. Seven slats at 3-1/2" wide consume 24-1/2" of the 37-3/4" depth set by the leg back pieces, which leaves 13-1/4" to divide over six gaps — about 2-1/4" between slats. Screw each slat down with two 2-1/2" screws into every leg it crosses; that is 42 screws for the slats alone, so plan on more than the sixteen the materials list carries. If you want a more finished look than bare slat ends, cover the exposed ends with plywood or planking pinned on with 1-1/4" brads — that is cosmetic only and carries no load.
materials: Wood screws, 2-1/2" or longer, for wall attachment

images.alt: Simple built-in daybed frame mounted to a wall
